fact: Check your feet every day. Look at the tops, bottoms, sides, heels, and between the toes. Use a mirror or ask someone for help if you cannot see the bottoms of your feet.
When checking your feet, look for cuts, sores, red spots, swelling, blisters, ingrown toenails, corns, calluses, plantar warts, athlete's foot, and unusually warm spots.
Contact your health care provider if a cut, sore, blister, or bruise on your foot does not begin to heal after a few days.
doctrine: educational — surfaces a 'discuss with your clinician' threshold, never a diagnosis

fact: Skin on the foot that is red, warm, or painful can be a sign of infection; tell your health care provider.
doctrine: educational — surfaces a 'discuss with your clinician' threshold, never a diagnosis

fact: A callus with dried blood inside it can be an early sign of a wound underneath; have it checked by your provider.
doctrine: educational — surfaces a 'discuss with your clinician' threshold, never a diagnosis

fact: Wash your feet with soap in warm — not hot — water. Test the water with a thermometer (90–95°F is safe) or your elbow, because diabetic nerve damage can keep you from feeling that water is too hot.
Do not soak your feet — soaking dries the skin. Dry feet well after washing, including between the toes.
Apply a thin coat of lotion, cream, or petroleum jelly to the tops and bottoms of your feet, but NOT between the toes — moisture between the toes can lead to infection.

Trim toenails after washing and drying, cutting straight across, and smooth with an emery board. Do not cut into the corners. Have a podiatrist trim them if you cannot see/feel/reach your feet or if nails are thick, yellowed, or curve into the skin.
Do not cut corns or calluses, and do not use corn plasters or liquid corn/callus removers — they can damage skin and cause infection. Ask your foot doctor about safe care.
Never walk barefoot or in socks alone — indoors or outdoors. Always wear shoes and socks to protect against injuries you may not feel.
Check the inside of your shoes before putting them on for pebbles, debris, rough seams, or torn linings. Buy shoes at the end of the day when feet are largest, and break new shoes in gradually.
Medicare Part B and some other insurance may help cover specially fitted (therapeutic) shoes and orthotics for people with diabetes.
Keep feet away from heaters and open fires, and do not use hot water bottles or heating pads on your feet — you may not feel a burn. Wear shoes on hot pavement and at the beach.

Help blood flow to your feet: prop your feet up when sitting, wiggle your toes and move your ankles for a few minutes several times a day, avoid tight socks, and do not smoke (smoking reduces blood flow to the feet).

Diabetic nerve damage (neuropathy) can cause tingling, pain, or loss of feeling in the feet. When you cannot feel your feet, you may not notice a pebble, blister, or cut — which can become infected and, with poor blood flow, heal slowly.

Ask your care team to check your feet at every visit (take off shoes and socks to remind them), and get a thorough foot exam — including checks of feeling and pulses — at least once a year. Get it at every visit if you have foot-shape changes, loss of feeling, peripheral artery disease, or a history of ulcers or amputation.

Structured foot self-care education and professional foot care should be repeated regularly based on risk: about every 1–3 months for people at high risk of ulceration and every 3–6 months for those at moderate risk.

For a neuropathic plantar (bottom-of-foot) ulcer, the preferred treatment is a non-removable knee-high offloading device (such as a total contact cast). If a removable device is used instead, it must be worn during all weight-bearing activity to heal.
doctrine: educational — describes standard of care, not a personal treatment instruction

fact: For people at moderate-to-high risk, wearing appropriate, properly fitted footwear during weight-bearing activity and frequently checking the skin for early (pre-ulcerative) lesions are core prevention steps.
